speaker: interviewer
text: Tape five. We're interviewing Demetrius Dvoichenko-Markov. It's March 21, 1996. And I'd like you to speak to me a little bit about your career over the last years, about your teaching and your writing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I went to three different universities. I went to UCLA, to Columbia, and to Rutgers. And I-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I did look for a job, naturally. And I got married while I was a graduate student. I met my wife at Columbia University. And the educational fields-- I took some courses at Teachers College. I was interested in American education.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I did have a job in the-- the Department of Defense, but I ran into some difficulties because I got a job to teach one course in geography at American University, and my security man, he said, you might reveal some top secrets that you are working on. And I said in geography? Oh, in geography. And he didn't like that I started publishing. I published an article. And it was thoroughly censored, but it dealt with Benjamin Franklin, which was not contemporary. So therefore, I decided to go into high school teaching, to teach Spanish and German. I taught elementary Spanish and German one, two, three. And I had a homeroom. So I got closer acquainted with the American education.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then after this experience, I was at a summer vacation in Connecticut with my in-laws, and I found a catalog of Monmouth College of New Jersey, which now is a university. And I decided to apply for a job there. And they right away called me long distance, come for an interview. So I came for an interview. My impression of New Jersey was the New Jersey Turnpike where they have those-- Secausus, those pig farms and so on. But I never heard about the Garden Parkway, a beautiful parkway, and here I get into a beautiful part of New Jersey, New Jersey Shore. And Monmouth College had several mansions. They have a mansion where the summer home of President Wilson used to be, and they have the mansion of Guggenheim. It was really beautiful, and the interview went all right. They say you can work for social science and introduce the teaching of Russian language.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was hired. And I returned back to Connecticut, told my wife about the job. She says New Jersey? Those pig farms? I said no, you have to see that area. So I brought her here and I had to tell her to close your mouth. She was so amazed at the beauty, you know, that is there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I started teaching at Monmouth College in 1957. And we moved from Washington. We moved to New Jersey. And it was a very nice place to raise children because of the beach nearby, and it was a lovely area. The only thing I found out, that you need a car. Because what happens is when I was given some night courses to teach and I had to walk about one mile to home, everybody lets their dogs loose. When-- when there's a little dog barking at you, it's OK, but then a huge Saint Bernard at night, you know, comes at you, you freeze. You don't know whether he's friendly or not. Well, he put his paws on my shoulder. I went down, and he started licking. I said oh yeah, thank god you are friendly. And after that I told my wife, I have to buy a car for the evening courses because they let--

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the initial college-- that was the first year that the college moved to the campus. It was very nice to see the college grow. And it did grow. And I was able, just during the time of Sputnik, there were a lot of students who wanted to learn Russian, you know, and I had a nice class there in Western civ. And then I taught Russian education, and I taught even a course on Byzantine history and Western civilization. So in a small college, you become a jack of all trades, you know? It was quite, quite, quite interesting. The students were very eager.

speaker: interviewee
text: The hardest time was during Vietnam War because we had-- the college was overcrowded with people. You had to write letters. One other thing that shocked us was a young fellow from Lithuania, he married and he graduated, and then he was drafted into the Army to go to Vietnam, and he was killed. So it was a personal experience because you knew him. That was a little bit hard. But now, the university is very big. I mean, I'm glad that I retired. And I have more to write. I-- I participated in many different international congresses, like a congress just now in September, I'm going to Vienna to the International Congress of Military History. And I'm going to talk about the Battle of Grunwald, Tannenberg in 1410, because they want medieval. And first time I went to Moscow was 1970. And I went to the International History Meeting in Moscow. And we had a shadow that followed us all over, even into the bathroom. And we got kind of scared, so we went to the American Embassy to register, just in case, because sometimes the communists deny the presence of anybody there. But we had all our lectures at-- at Moscow University. And they said OK, your turn will come to talk about your paper. And I had wrote a paper on the Romanian-Russian relations in the 19th century. And they said OK, you belong to that group. You will present your paper there. And he says everybody will have about 20 minutes just to give the resume of their paper.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I patiently waited there. And I took my children with me, too. And what they did is when the delegate from People's Republic of Poland came in, instead of 20 minutes, he talked about 3/4 of an hour or an hour. Then the delegate from Bulgaria, People's Republic and so on. And the time was out, and they froze me off. But one American from California, he says listen. I came here over from California. Whether you listen or not, I'm going to read my paper. I couldn't do that because I had my kids with me, you know? So I didn't know what repercussions were. Now, this paper was published here in Vienna, finally this paper was published. But the Romanian delegation told me, they wouldn't like your paper because it's not their point of view, you know? [LAUGHS] So I-- I did go. I was invited several times to Romania because I grew up there, under the communist dictator Ceausescu. And they gave me a chauffeur, drive the car, a Volga, to see any part of Romania that I wanted, like the monasteries and so on. And I also presented some papers there, in Romania.

speaker: interviewee
text: In fact, we were invited to the Republic of Moldova where I grew up. And I was the official guest of the Supreme Soviet of Moldova. They did-- they were enthusiastic about the American delegation because the American delegation always presents the paper in the English language. But we noticed that not too many people speak English. And since the American delegation knew the Romanian language, we decided to give the resume in Romania. Boy, you should see the ovation we got. We were on TV and so on. It was very, very, very interesting to exchange points of view. And I have about almost 40 articles published in different professional journals. And there were some celebrations here in New York. They have a New York library. And they asked me to-- also Free Europe asked me to participate with them. So it was a very, very interesting experience, which is continued even today. I had to go, like, we have a Slavic meeting in Rutgers University. I presented a paper there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There are some papers pending publication still. So I gave you the whole list there. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: How has the Nuremberg experience impacted your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, I'm disappointed that things that we hoped for did not happen, that so many criminals did escape and-- like Wernher von Braun, right? He said Deutschland, my country, right or wrong. And so I'm disappointed that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we didn't achieve what we wanted. I'm very, well, I mean, you might say disappointed because what we expected to be the impact of the Nuremberg trial did not happen, and the same crimes are being committed today, in the name of security or name of different other names that are being used today, but the crimes are still there. And I don't know what's going to happen in Hague. We are a little bit one-sided that we blame the Serbs for everything but we hide the crimes of the Croats. In fact, the files of the Croats were stolen about the war crimes committed by them. Like for example, the Croats blew up a whole monastery and hanged all the priests. This is not mentioned by our-- by our press. So I think it's one-sided. We tried to be, at Nuremberg, evenhanded and assume that everybody is not guilty until proven guilty. And we tried to prove they are guilt beyond any doubt. I hope we did a good job. We tried. All of us tried to-- to-- to do a good job. Now I don't know whatever to do at Hague. We will see. So far they have only a Serbian general there that was captured, and I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you kept in touch with the people you met in Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, particularly with one man that was my housemate, that lived in the same house with me. And he used to live in Annandale. And every time that we came to Washington, we got together. In fact, we were planning to get together here. And unfortunately, he died in January of a massive heart attack. And he was such a-- a jogger and healthy, but you can't predict those things. It's a little bit sad. My two bosses, the lawyers that I worked with closely, they died, too. But one of them, during the first meeting of Nuremberg, I met him here. But the-- the other one died before. So slowly our ranks are thinning. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you speak often and publicly about your involvement in Nuremberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I just spoke for-- to Hadassah. And I spoke at the synagogue. So I do try to spread the word. Even when I was working at Monmouth College, I also gave the students a little information about Nuremberg trial. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: In 1950, there was a clemency board that reviewed some of the Nuremberg sentences, and there were some sentence reductions.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was disappointed in the Krupp trial, that it was stopped in the middle and he was free. There where rumors that Ilse Koch, she was sentenced to death because she was beating the prisoners and using their tattoo to make lampshades. And she was sentenced to death at Dachau by the American War Crimes. But she managed to get pregnant and gave birth to a black baby. So General Clay who was in charge of the American occupation, leaving for Christmas, pardoned her, and I heard that she was even getting a pension. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I don't know [LAUGHS] what's going on. [LAUGHS] I don't know. There are a lot of rumors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything you would like to add at this point, anything that you forgot to mention that you'd like to add now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I hope that we-- we will be able to stop war crimes. I hope that maybe the American Army that is now in Yugoslavia might do something to stop this killing. Because people in their nature are friendly to each other. I saw in Yugoslavia. I traveled several times. And I saw that people of different faith and different-- they-- they-- they-- they got along very nicely, and now suddenly, they're killing each other. It's-- it's-- it's horrible, those-- those things that are going on. And maybe people will learn not to do it again, that live-- live in peace. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know. Like for example, they say that the communists don't exist anymore because they changed their name to a democratic party in Hungary. But the communists are still in charge. In Romania, the same thing. So I don't know. But what happened is that the leadership, democratic leadership was killed off. And great numbers-- I know from Romanian history that many innocent intellectuals were killed so that the-- the opposition wouldn't have any leadership. Or they would divide the opposition into small parties, many, many small parties so that they wouldn't get any votes to take over, and the communists still can control. Even the Church is being divided, too, to fight each other. Like for example, Gorbachev went to see the Pope. And he declared the law of 1948 null and void, which did do away with the so-called [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] church that recognized the Pope, Uniat church, in the 16th century. And that was done away with in order to reestablish the Orthodox control. Now the Soviet government has, again, revived the Uniats and you have a fight between the Uniats and the Orthodox. So even the church is divided. I read the time just recently that the patriarch of Moscow comes from Estonia, and the Estonians don't want to be under the patriarch of Moscow. They want to be under the patriarch of Constantinople. So again, a division of the church, not only national division. And it's funny that the Muslims of-- of Yugoslavia speak exactly the same language as the Serbs. And the Croats speak exactly the same language. So they say they have their common origin. And the reason the Muslims turned to the Muslim faith is not to pay taxes. That's why they turned on the Turkish occupation, because Turkish occupation was even charging taxes of children. Five-year-old sons of a family would be taken away by the Turks from the family, sent to Constantinople, and raised as fanatical Muslims, who would die for Muhammad and for the Sultan. They became Janissaries. And this tax was called devshirme.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people in-- in Eastern Europe cannot forget that, when your child is taken away. And you will find some homes in Eastern Europe that don't have any chimney. And you say what's the matter, why the home doesn't have any chimney? The Turks had a tax on smoke. So therefore they had the smoke in the attic, not to pay the tax. You want to-- to visit an old church that was built during Turkish occupation, you have to go steps down because the church is in a hole. And I say my goodness, why don't you build it on even ground? Oh, we wanted to have a bell tower. I say, so what's the matter with a bell tower? Well, there was a Turkish law during Turkish occupation that the bell tower is not allowed to be taller than the minaret of a mosque. So therefore we're digging a ditch in order to have a bell tower. [LAUGHS] So people still remember, and I think we will have trouble. Yeah, we will have trouble. Already one of the soldiers was wounded, right, by-- So I-- I hope that maybe past history might help. But you know also, if you study history, you will see that people make exactly the same mistakes that they made in the past. I was teaching in Western civilization about Napoleon and Hitler. Both of them wanted to conquer Russia or the Soviet Union. Both of them thought that one summer is enough to completely conquer Russia and the Russians. But they didn't know how to make friends. Because what did they do? Napoleon, when he marched in, the peasants heard about the French Revolution-- fraternite, egalite, and so on. And they wanted to turn against their landowners, against the Russian aristocracy. Napoleon wanted to deal with the aristocracy. So he sent his army to put down these uprisings. This way he turned the majority of the people against him. The same thing Hitler did, too, because the majority wanted to get rid of the communists. And I saw a document in Nuremberg, a top secret document signed by Hitler. And it said that this document should be destroyed after executing this order. And you know what the order was? The order was to the German generals on the Eastern Front, who were using native Russians to fight against the communists. They formed such auxiliary units to the Wehrmacht, and they performed wonderful. The generals were very pleased because they knew how to pick up the communists and so on. Now, Hitler issued an order. All these auxiliary units should be executed as bandits. I saw the document myself. It seems that one German general didn't destroy this document. He was captured by us and we had this document in Nuremberg. Do you think that created a lot of friends? Because many of the people who fought against the communists, they felt that the German generals wouldn't do that, execute the order. But the Germans are inculcated right from the beginning to follow orders. That's why we were told that if you are at the front fighting the Germans, pick up the officers. Once they don't have the officers, they surrender, because they don't know what to do because they're used to follow orders.

speaker: interviewee
text: In our army it's different. The sergeant is the one [LAUGHS] who is important in our American Army. In fact, during maneuvers I remember one young officer who was arguing with a roadblock that we encountered, and he was taken prisoner. And the sergeant took over. He says boys, we bypass this block. And we were able to drive in the trucks back to the camp. The officer had to walk. He was punished by arguing with the enemy. [LAUGHS] He had to go through, you know. So I mean, it's a different psychology that you have. It's entirely different psychology.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the same-- so the same thing happened. He turned the people against him. That's why the Germans failed. That's why the French failed, too. It's exactly the same date. And I hope we won't fail. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I thank you very, very much for taking this time with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This the coat of arms for the Markov family, which goes all the way up to 1200. And my great-grandfather was the Marshal of Nobility in the Don Cossack Host. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] First, the family were boyars in Moscow. They were persecuted by Ivan by the Terrible. They had to flee to the frontier to survive the frontier. Then the communists came. We had to flee to America, [LAUGHS] and from here, where do we go? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This-- this is my mother. This is the refugee camp in Greece, in Thessaloniki, Greece. And that's me in an American [INAUDIBLE], because the camp was run by the American Red Cross and the Greek Red Cross.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Alaska where I was an interpreter of the US Army. And I'm here with my colleagues, who are members of my outfit.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where-- who are you in this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's me. 0

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- you have to say.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm--

speaker: interviewee
text: Far left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Far left.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're on the left with the hat?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left, with a  hat on, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a commendation for the service in Alaska given by the Alaska Defense Command during World War II to each one of us who served in Alaska. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the identification card issued by the war crimes in Nuremberg. Without this identification card, you couldn't enter into the courthouse. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture is where I worked at Nuremberg courthouse. And the boxes that you see there are captured documents that we were working on to get the evidence against the war criminals.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what year was this when this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Around 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and where it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Nuremberg, Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Nuremberg, Germany. It's the big square, Market Platz. And here, you can see a church that only a shell remained after our bombing of the Nuremberg city. And I'm right in front of it. So that's the way it looked when I was at the Nuremberg trial. Now it has been rebuilt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year do you think this was taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a billboard put by the US Army on the German border. No fraternization with Germans is allowed. It's written there. It's in '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Nuremberg, Germany. It's a hotel, Grand Hotel, where was our meeting place after work. It was being rebuilt by US Army engineers, as you can see the scaffolding there. Now, it's a beautiful hotel today. But at that time it was in ruins, and that's where we got together. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What's this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of me and my coworkers at the Nuremberg trial, from different delegations. And you can see me there, kneeling down right in front of the group. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is '47. This picture was taken '47. It's the American chaplain of the Nuremberg trial visiting the wife and the daughter of Goering to help them out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- who is the--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the chaplain, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kneeling in front?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kneeling in front.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who the other people are, starting from the left.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a fellow who worked in my office. On the left is [INAUDIBLE], [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a former professor who was working also at the Nuremberg trial.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then next to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is the wife of Goering.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then next to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: This fellow I don't know exactly who it is. I know that--

speaker: interviewer
text: And on the far right?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know who's on the far right. I got this from the chaplain.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was issued to us by-- in Nuremberg to facilitate our visit to different occupation zones. It was sort of a passport.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a-- a statement from our personnel at the war crimes in Nuremberg about the jobs that I was assigned to, the main jobs. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in court. On the left is a witness, a Ukrainian girl that knew only Ukrainian, at the trial against Krupp. And I was assigned as an interpreter. I am on the right. This is at the Krupp Trial. And this is another Ukrainian witness that was used as a slave laborer in the Krupp Enterprises. She spoke only Ukrainian and I was assigned as her interpreter on the-- on the right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a trial of the SS administration. And this is the man in charge of the SS extermination, Oswald Pohl. I was very active working on this trial. And in the background there is my friend that just recently died.
